plainMeaning: Three lovely white umbrellas, crores of gods waving fans on every side, and a self-sounding heavenly drum surround the Lord — this dazzling display of his marvels delights absolutely everyone.
recitation: ātapatra-trayaṁ cāru-vaśyāmarāḥ, koṭi-saṁkhyā bhajante 'bhita-śyāmarāḥ | anupahata-dundubhi-dhvanitam acyuta-pade, yad-atiśaya-rājir eṣā na keṣāṁ mude ||

wordByWord.gloss: (white) umbrella, parasol — the royal-spiritual canopy held over an Arhat
wordByWord.etymology: ātapa (heat, sunshine) + tra (that which protects, from √trai 'to shield') = 'sun-shield'
wordByWord.notes: Here one of the eight prātihārya; the three-tiered ātapatra marks a Jina's spiritual sovereignty.

wordByWord.gloss: a triad, the set of three (the threefold umbrella)
wordByWord.etymology: from tri ('three') + -aya; neuter trayam = 'a group of three'
wordByWord.notes: The triple/tiered umbrella (ātapatra-traya) named in the preface among the divine marks.

wordByWord.gloss: beautiful, and the devoted gods (lit. 'lovely; obedient deathless ones')
wordByWord.etymology: cāru ('beautiful') + vaśya ('subject, obedient', from √vaś) + amarāḥ ('the deathless ones = gods', a- 'not' + mara 'death')
wordByWord.notes: A yamaka cluster chiming with pāda 2's '...śyāmarāḥ'. cāru qualifies the umbrellas; vaśya-amarāḥ = the submissive gods who attend. Exact morphological split is poetry-driven; meaning is secure even where the surface saṁdhi is ornamental.

wordByWord.etymology: koṭi ('point, tip; the highest number, ten million')

wordByWord.notes: koṭi-saṁkhyā = 'crores in number', describing the attending amarāḥ.

wordByWord.gloss: they attend/serve [him] on every side — the gods (amarāḥ) from all directions
wordByWord.etymology: bhajante (3rd pl. ātmanepada of √bhaj, 'they resort to, honour, attend') + abhitaḥ ('on both/all sides', from abhi) + amarāḥ ('gods'); the avagraha (ઽ) marks elided initial a- of abhitaḥ
wordByWord.notes: The yamaka deliberately echoes pāda 1's ending; sense: the deathless gods serve the Lord encircling him, waving the cāmara fly-whisks.

wordByWord.gloss: unstruck, unbeaten — i.e. sounding of its own accord
wordByWord.notes: The divine dundubhi resounds though no hand strikes it — a mark of the self-arising celestial drum.

wordByWord.etymology: onomatopoeic dundubhi ('kettle-drum'); the book's spelling 'huṁdubhi' is preserved verbatim
wordByWord.notes: One of the aṣṭa-prātihārya; its boom proclaims the Jina's sermon across the worlds.

wordByWord.etymology: past participle of √dhvan ('to sound, resound'); neuter dhvanitam absorbs the following m by saṁdhi
wordByWord.notes: anupahata-dundubhi-dhvanitam = 'the resounding of the unstruck drum'.

wordByWord.etymology: (m = saṁdhi-glide) + acyuta ('unfallen, imperishable', a- + √cyu 'to fall') + pade (locative of pada, 'place, station')
wordByWord.notes: 'Around the imperishable Lord' (acyuta as the unfailing Jina) is the primary reading; a secondary reading takes acyuta-pada as the heavens, the drum's boom reaching even the high abodes. Flagged in notes.

wordByWord.etymology: yat (relative 'which, whose') + atiśaya ('surpassing eminence, marvel'; ati 'beyond' + √śī/śri); in Jain usage atiśaya = a Tīrthaṅkara's supernatural distinctions
wordByWord.notes: Introduces the relative clause: 'this row of the Lord's marvels, which...'.

wordByWord.etymology: rāji ('line, row, streak', also punning on √rāj 'to shine') + eṣā (fem. 'this'); the visarga of rājiḥ becomes -r before eṣā
wordByWord.notes: rāji carries a built-in glow-pun: 'this shining series' of attributes.
With keṣāṁ forms the rhetorical 'whose [joy] not?' = everyone's.

wordByWord.etymology: genitive plural of kim ('who, what'): keṣām
wordByWord.notes: na keṣāṁ = 'of whom is it not' = 'of none is it not' = of all.

wordByWord.etymology: dative of mud ('joy, delight', from √mud 'to rejoice')
wordByWord.notes: Dative of purpose: 'serves for the joy of'. The closing rhetorical question affirms universal delight.

literalTranslation: The threefold umbrella is lovely; gods, obedient and crores in number, attend [the Lord] on every side; the unstruck divine drum is sounded at the imperishable station — this shining array of [his] marvels, for whose joy is it not?
idiomaticTranslation: Three exquisite white parasols arch above him; crores of devoted gods throng on every side, fans waving; and the self-sounding celestial drum thunders around the imperishable Lord. Whose heart could this radiant procession of marvels fail to gladden?

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding verse showed the saṅgha drinking the nectar of teaching that cools souls scorched in saṁsāra, this verse lifts the eye from the audience to the Jina's own person and the wonders that crown it. The poet names, in rapid succession, three of the divine marks: the triple umbrella (ātapatra-traya), the fly-whisks implied in the crores of gods who 'attend on every side' (bhajante 'bhitaḥ — the cāmara-bearers), and the unstruck drum (anupahata-dundubhi) that sounds though no hand touches it. The selection is deliberate: umbrella above, chowries around, drum resounding through space — a vertical, lateral, and sonic envelopment of the Lord. The verse is saturated with yamaka: the two half-lines chime on '...vaśyāmarāḥ / ...śyāmarāḥ', and rāji puns on √rāj 'to shine', so the 'row' of marvels literally glitters in the word. The clinching device is the rhetorical question na keṣāṁ mude — 'for whose joy is it not?' — a litotes that, by denying any exception, asserts the universal, irresistible delight these marvels evoke. Note the gods are vaśya, 'obedient': even the deathless serve the Jina, an inversion the verse savours. acyuta-pade fixes the scene at the 'imperishable station', most naturally the unfailing Lord himself at the centre of the samavasaraṇa.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse versifies part of the aṣṭa-prātihārya — the eight involuntary signs that attend an Arhat after kevala-jñāna. Doctrinally these are not magic the Jina performs but karmically-conditioned splendours that arise around a being of his attainment; the Jina is vītarāga and does nothing to summon them. Hence the telling detail that the drum is anupahata, 'unstruck': it sounds by the natural fitness of the moment, not by an agent, mirroring how a Tīrthaṅkara's very presence orders the cosmos without effort. The crores of obedient devas embody the Jain hierarchy in which even heavenly beings, still bound in saṁsāra, revere one who has conquered it. The umbrella's threefold form is traditionally read as sovereignty over the three worlds. atiśaya names precisely this class of supernal distinctions of a Jina.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Chanting this verse, the reciter mentally enters the samavasaraṇa and stands among the crores of attending gods — a deliberate cultivation of bahumāna (reverential awe). The closing question 'whose joy is it not?' invites self-examination: if even the deathless gods drop their pride to serve, the reciter checks his own ego before the Jina. The image steadies a wandering mind in kāyotsarga by giving it a luminous, ordered scene to rest on rather than the day's anxieties.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭa-mahāprātihārya — the eight divine attributes (umbrella, cāmara, dundubhi, aśoka tree, siṁhāsana, bhāmaṇḍala, divya-dhvani, puṣpa-vṛṣṭi)
crossReferences.source: Standard Jain prātihārya lists; cf. Tiloyapaṇṇatti and Abhidhāna-rājendra-koṣa s.v. pāḍihera

crossReferences.text: Companion prātihārya imagery in the Laghu Ajitaśānti Stava (8th Smaraṇa) of Vīragaṇin
crossReferences.source: Anchalgachchha Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit

sources.title: Ātapatra — definitions (Sanskrit/Hinduism)
sources.consultedFor: Sense and etymology of ātapatra ('sun-shielding parasol') and its iconographic use as a canopy of honour.

sources.title: Dundubhi — 43 definitions incl. Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of dundubhi as the great celestial proclamation-drum and its Jain prātihārya context.

sources.title: Atiśaya — 28 definitions incl. Jain philosophy
sources.consultedFor: Technical Jain sense of atiśaya as a Tīrthaṅkara's surpassing supernatural distinctions.

sources.title: Prātihārya — definitions incl. Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Confirming prātihārya as the class of divine attributes (umbrella, cāmara, dundubhi) attending an Arhat.

notes: The verse is in heavy yamaka, so the two half-line endings 'cāru-vaśy-amarāḥ' and '(a)bhita-śyāmarāḥ' chime phonetically and their exact morphological segmentation is ornamental rather than strictly grammatical; the doctrinal sense (umbrellas + attending/fanning gods) is nonetheless secure. The book's spelling 'huṁdubhi' (હુંદુભિ) for dundubhi and the avagraha in 'bhajante'bhita...' are preserved verbatim per the source. 'acyuta-pade' is read primarily as 'at/around the imperishable Lord'; a defensible secondary reading takes acyuta-pada as the heavens whose denizens hear the drum — left open rather than forced. The chowrie (cāmara) is present by implication (the crores of attending gods), not by an explicit cāmara-token. The cross-reference editions (Tiloyapaṇṇatti, Abhidhāna-rājendra) are cited by title for the prātihārya doctrine, not for this verse's wording.
